Facts.text: Roman Emperor Julian defeats the Sassanid army in the Battle of Ctesiphon, under the walls of the Sassanid capital, but is unable to take the city
St Paul I begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Anti-Pope Victor IV (Gregorio) submits himself to Pope Innocentius II
Battle of Monte Porzio: Holy Roman army supporting Pope Alexander III is defeated by Christian of Buch and Rainald of Dassel
Chinese city of Kaifeng, capital of the Jurchen Jin dynasty, surrenders to the Mongols under General Subedei after a siege of more than a year
Pope John XXIII [Baldassare Cossa] formally deposed as Pope at the Conference of Constance, Germany, after he had fled the town in disguise
Battle of Gallipoli: The Venetian fleet under Commander Pietro Loredan defeat and capture a large part of the Ottoman fleet off the Gallipoli port
French banker Jacques Coeur's possessions confiscated
Spanish army under Cristóbal de Mondragón conquers Zierik sea
The Korean navy, led by Admiral Yi Sun-sin, repels a Japanese fleet in the Battle of Sacheon, the first use of a Korean Turtle Ship
Treaty of Middle Plantation establishes peace between the Virginia colonists and local Native Americans
Battle at La Hogue: English and Dutch fleet beat France
Royal Hospital Chelsea Founder's Day first celebrated in London
First mastectomy performed in North America at Hôtel-Dieu de Québec by royal doctor Michel Sarrazin on Sister Marie Barbier de l’Assomption (operation is successful)
South Carolina formally incorporated as a royal colony
Peter II becomes Tsar of Russia aged 11
The right of Canadians to keep Indian slaves is upheld in Quebec City
Battle of Waxhaw Creek: alleged massacre of 113 of Colonel Abraham Buford's continentals by Lieutenant Colonel Banastre Tarleton's troops after the continentals raised a white flag
Battle at Curtazone: Austrians beat Sardinia-Piemonte
Wisconsin becomes 30th US state
Lincoln says "You can fool some of the people all of the time, and all of the people some of the time, but you can not fool all of the people all of the time."
Mexican Emperor Maximilian arrives at Vera Cruz
Michael Obrenovich III, Prince of Serbia, is assassinated in Belgrade
Present constitution of Switzerland takes effect
Europe's first steam cable trams start in Highgate, London
August Strindberg's "Hemsoborna" premieres in Copenhagen
Trademark "Escalator" registered by Otis Elevator Co.
Dutch State Mine law forms
May coup d'etat: Alexander Obrenovich, King of Serbia, and Queen Draga, are assassinated in Belgrade by the Black Hand (Crna Ruka) organization
Pogrom against Jewish community in Brisk, Lithuania
Frank "Home Run" Baker's 1st career home run for Philadelphia Athletics
Pope Pius X's encyclical on Editae Saepe, against church reformers
Ballets Russes premieres their ballet L'après-midi d'un faune (The Afternoon of a Faun) in Paris, choreographed by Vaslav Nijinsky
Fifteen young women are fired by Curtis Publishing in Philadelphia for dancing the "Turkey Trot" during their lunch break
Norwegian ship Storstad collides with Canadian ship Empress of Ireland on St Lawrence River; 1,024 die
NY Giants win 17th consecutive road game
Official flag of President of the United States adopted
US forces invade Dominican Republic, stay until 1924

The Republic of Prekmurje founded - a short-lived, unrecognised state, which on June 6, 1919 was incorporated into the newly established Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (renamed "Yugoslavia" in 1929)
US Supreme Court rules that organized baseball is a sport and not a business, thus not subject to antitrust laws
AEK Athens FC is established on the anniversary of the siege of Constantinople by the Turks
Fritz von Opel reaches 200 kph in experimental rocket car
The Bonus Army of World War I veterans begins to assemble in Washington, D.C. to request cash bonuses promised to them to be paid in 1945
French liner Normandie begins its maiden voyage, arrived in NYC on June 3rd
Hague local museum opens
Adolf Kiefer swims world record 100 yards backstroke (58.8 sec)
Arthur Seyss-Inquart installed as Reich commissar of The Hague, Netherlands
In WWII, Germans capture Ostend & Ypres in Belgium and Lille in France
Meat and cheese rationed in US
British troops occupy Aprilia, Italy
US 1st Marine division conquers Shuri Castle, Okinawa
KVP wins Provincial National election in Netherlands
Candid Camera, TV comedy show, moves to NBC
First North Pole flight in a single-engine plane completed by Charles F. Blair Jr., who flies 3,260 nonstop miles across the North Pole from Bardufoss, Norway, to Fairbanks, Alaska
2nd Round Conference between Dutch Antilles & Suriname ends
500th anniversary of the fall of Constantinople and the end of the Byzantine Empire (to OttomanSultan Mehmed II)
British runner Diane Leather becomes first woman to run the mile in under 5 minutes; 4:59.6 at Alexander Sports Ground in Birmingham, England
First of the annual Bilderberg conferences, fostering relations between Europe and North America held at Oosterbeek, Netherlands
Pope Pius X is canonized a saint by Pope Pius XII
Jordan government of Tewfik Abdul Huda resigns
WESH TV channel 2 in Daytona Beach-Orlando, FL (NBC) 1st broadcast
Algerian rebels kill 336 collaborators
Laos government of prince Suvanna Phuma resigns
NYC Mayor Robert Wagner says he plans to confer with the Giants and Dodgers baseball teams about the proposed move to the west coast
Saunders-Roe SR.N1, the first practical hovercraft, performs its first engine run
4th Grammy Awards: "Moon River", Peter Nero win
MLB Philadelphia Phillies Dick Allen hits 529' HR out of Connie Mack Stadium in Philadelphia
Australian Paul McManus water skis barefoot for 1:30:19
UN resolves sanctions on white-minority-ruled Rhodesia
US Truth in Lending Act signed into law
Atlantic Records releases the eponymous debut album of "Crosby, Stills & Nash"; it contains hits  'Marrakesh Express' and 'Suite: Judy Blue Eyes'
Britain's Trans-Arctic expedition makes 1st crossing of Arctic Sea ice
General strike in Cordoba, Argentina, leading to the Cordobazo civil unrest
USSR performs underground nuclear test
"Court Room" by Clarence Carter hits #61
The Official IRA announce a ceasefire
Columbia Records fires president Clive Davis for misappropriating $100,000 in funds, Davis will start Arista records
Thomas Bradley elected 1st African American mayor of Los Angeles, California
Northern Ireland is brought under direct rule from Westminster
Car tune "One Piece At A Time" byJohnny Cashhits #29
Only HR of Joe Niekro's 22-year career, comes off brother Phil
Australian Sue Prell first female golfer to hit consecutive holes-in one; 13th and 14th holes at Chatswood Golf Club, Sydney
NBC News & Information Service (24-hour news) ends on radio
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
US first-class postage rises 3 cents to 15 cents after being held static for three years
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
CBS premiere of Melissa Anderson's modern "Survival of Dana", her first "bad girl" role, in which Anderson's title character leaves her North Dakota town for Los Angeles after her parents' divorce
Radio's first rock network "The Source" premieres
53rd National Spelling Bee: Jacques Bailly wins spelling elucubrate
Attempted assassination on Vernon Jordan Jr National Urban League president
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
"I Know What Boys Like" by The Waitresses hits #62
First papal visit to Britain since 1531
Pentagon plans first strategy to fight a nuclear war
Yuri Dumchev of USSR throws discus a record 71.86 m
Amputee Steve Fonyo completes cross-Canada marathon at Victoria, British Columbia, after 14 months
59th National Spelling Bee: Jon Pennington wins spelling odontalgia
Jury finds "Twilight Zone: The Movie" director John Landis innocent of involuntary manslaughter in death of actor Vic Morrow and 2 child actors during filming
Robin Ventura set a college baseball record with hits in 57 games
Graeme Hick scores his 1,000th run of 1st-class cricket season
Pakistan President Zia ul-Haq dismisses government and disbands parliament
An earthquake hits Peru, killing over 200
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits a record 2,870.49
MLB New York Mets fire manager Davey Johnson and hire Bud Harrelson as his replacement
MLB New York Mets score in 9th inning to end home shut-out streak at 3 games
Neo-Nazis kill 5 Turkish women in Solingen, Germany
Great iceball comet seen above North Sea
Hungary's Socialist Party wins parliamentary election
70th National Spelling Bee: Rebecca Sealfon wins spelling euonym
Spanish scientists announce a new human species in a 780,000-year-old fossil
Olusegun Obasanjo takes office as President of Nigeria, the first elected and civilian head of state in Nigeria after 16 years of military rule
Space Shuttle Discovery completes the first docking with the International Space Station
International Day of United Nations Peacekeepers is inaugurated
Natural gas futures plunge 6% to a 10-month low on speculation that growing US inventories will help power plants meet summer demand for air-conditioning
U.S. Supreme Court rules that disabled golfer Casey Martin can use a cart to ride in tournaments
The Al-Khobar massacres in Saudi Arabia kill 22
World War II Memorial is dedicated in Washington, D.C.
France resoundingly rejects the European Constitution

In an all South African Super 14 Rugby final, the Bulls (Pretoria) retain title, 25-17 over the Stormers (Cape Town) at the Orlando Stadium, Soweto; Morné Steyn kicks 6 penalties and a conversion for the Bulls
Philadelphia Phillies' Roy Halladay becomes 20th pitcher to throw a perfect game (1-0 vs Florida Marlins), in Miami
Super Rugby Final, Orlando Stadium, Soweto: Bulls (Pretoria) successfully defend their title with a 25-17 win over the Stormers (Cape Town) in an all-South African final
5.9 magnitude earthquake kills 24 people near Bologna, Italy
Facebook's problematic public listing could cost those involved $115 million from technical glitches
Indonesian police seize over a million ecstasy pills, valued at $45 million; their biggest drug bust in ten years
Thousands march in protest in Johannesburg against Brett Murray's controversial painting "The Spear", part of the artist's Hail to the Thief II exhibition  that criticizes South Africa's ruling party
Heat wave in India centered in Telangana and Andhra Pradesh states is reported to have killed 1800 people in a week
Violent storm and high winds in Moscow, Russia leaves 13 dead
Death toll on Puerto Rico 70 times higher than official figure, likely 4,600 died from Hurricane Maria according to Harvard University study
Heatwave in Karachi, Pakistan, kills 65 as temperatures soar above 110 degrees Fahrenheit
Missouri Governor Eric Greitens resigns over an extramarital affair and illegal use of a donor list
Russian journalist Arkady Babchenko fakes his own death with Ukrainian security services to foil an assassination plot
Starbucks closes more than 8,000 US stores early for racial bias training after two black men wrongly arrested in a store in April
16 people charged for setting fire to and murdering a teenager who reported sexual harassment at an Islamic school in Feni, Bangladesh
Tourist boat sinks after colliding with another boat on the Danube River in Budapest, Hungary, killing 28 people
Transgender no longer classified as a mental health illness by the World Health Organization
World's smallest surviving baby, a girl, discharged from Sharp March Birch Hospital in San Diego after being born at 23 weeks weighing 8.6 ounces (245 grams)
At least 79 people reported killed in heavy rains and landslides in the Brazilian northeastern province of Pernambuco, with 56 more missing
Jai Hindley becomes only the second Australian cyclist to win a Grand Tour with a 1' 18" victory over 2019 champion Richard Carapaz in the Giro d'Italia
Man disguised as an elderly disabled woman throws cake at the Mona Lisa at the Louvre in Paris, painting undamaged
Canadian hiker Delaney Irving (19) wins perilous Cooper's Hill Cheese-Rolling in Brockworth, Glocestershire, England, despite being knocked unconscious
Extreme heatwave in northern and central India with unconfirmed record temperature reading of 52.3 C (126.14 F), later downgraded to 49 C (120.2 F)

Canadian provinces of Manitoba and Saskatchewan declare states of emergency due to rapidly expanding wildfires; over 160 fires have burned at least 1.56 million acres and resulted in evacuation of more than 18,000 people
Faizan Zaki (13) of Plano, Texas wins the Scripps National Spelling Bee in National Harbor, Maryland; final word was
Battle at Legnano: Lombard League beatsFrederick IBarbarossa and the Holy Roman Empire
French KingPhilip VIof Valois crowned at the Cathedral in Reims, France
Constantinople, capital of the Eastern Roman Empire, falls to the Ottoman Turks underMehmed the Conqueror, ending the Byzantine Empire after 1,100 years
John Winthropbegins "History of New England"
KingCharles Iof England attempts to escape captivity at Carisbrooke Castle, Isle of Wight, for second time by hacksawing through a barred window but caught in the act
Battle of Goodwin Sands, off Folkestone, Kent: English 'General at Sea' Robert Blake drives out Dutch fleet under Lieutenant AdmiralMaarten Tromp
Battle of Samugarh: decisive battle of the Mughal succession won byAurangzeband his brothers against their brother and heir-apparent Dara Shikoh, near Agra
On his 30th birthday,Charles IIreturns to London from exile in the Netherlands to claim the English throne after the Puritan Commonwealth comes to an end
Joseph Haydn's first opera "Krumme Teufel" (The Limping Devil) premieres in Vienna
Patrick Henry's historic speech against the Stamp Act, answering a cry of "Treason!" with, "If this be treason, make the most of it!"
"Virginia Plan" byJames MadisonandEdmund Randolphproposed to the Constitutional Convention advocating for a national government with three branches - legislative, executive, and judicial
Sojourner Truthaddresses the first Black Women's Rights Convention in Akron, Ohio
Paddington Station, London's terminus for the Great Western Railway opens, with a design byIsambard Kingdom Brunel
Dorothea Dixoffers help in setting up hospitals for the Union Army
American pharmacistJohn Pembertonbegins advertising his patented medicine, Coca-Cola, in Atlanta, Georgia
Ignacy Jan Paderewski's opera "Manru" has its world premiere in Dresden, Germany
Igor Stravinsky's avant-garde ballet score "Le Sacre du Printemps" (The Rite of Spring) for the Ballets Russes premieres at the Théâtre des Champs-Élysées in Paris, provoking a riot

Albert Einstein's Theory of Relativity, that when light passes a large body, gravity will bend the rays confirmed by Arthur Eddington's expedition to photograph a solar eclipse on the island of Principe, West Africa
Charles Stritefiles patent for the automatic pop-up toaster
"Yankee Doodle Dandy," based on the life ofGeorge M. Cohan, directed by Michael Curtiz and starringJames Cagneyand Joan Leslie, premieres in NYC (Academy Award for Best Actor 1943)

Bing CrosbyrecordsIrving Berlin's song "White Christmas" with the John Scott Trotter Orchestra and the Ken Darby Singers in just 18 minutes; it becomes the world's best-selling single, with an estimated 100 million copies sold
Algiers Allied Planning Conference betweenWinston Churchill, George Marshall and GeneralDwight D. Eisenhowerfinalizes the plan for the Italian Campaign
TheCarter Familydebut on the Grand Ole Opry with Chet Atkins
PopePius XIIissues holy declaration to canonize former Pope Pius X as a saint in the Catholic church
Arnold Schoenberg's "Modern Psalm" premieres
Charles de Gaulleforms French government
Everly Brothers single "Cathy's Clown" hits #1
"A Raisin in the Sun", film adaptation of Lorraine Hansberry's stage play, starring the Broadway cast includingSidney Poitier, Ruby Dee, John Fiedler, and Ivan Dixon premieres
Barbra Streisandappears on "The Garry Moore Show"
American long jumperRalph Bostonsets then world record 27' 4 3/4" (8.35m) at Modesto, California
ElvisandPriscilla Presleyhold a wedding reception at Graceland for friends and family not at the original ceremony
JMW Turner's oil painting "Juliet and Her Nurse" sold for $6,400,000 in NYC
Larry Birdbeats outMagic Johnsonfor NBA rookie of year
Boston Red Sox retires #9 (Ted Williams) & #4 (Joe Cronin)
British pop singerFreddie Mercuryand Spanish opera diva Montserrat Caballé debut their song "Barcelona" at the Ibiza Fest
Michael Jacksonattempts to buy the remains of "The Elephant Man," Joseph Merrick
Philadelphia Phillies 12-time All Star 3rd basemanMike Schmidtretires from MLB at 39
Boris Yeltsinis elected President of the Russian Republic
MLB Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonsteals record 893rd base, breakingTy Cobb's record
MLB Chicago White SoxTim Rainesswipes his 700th career base
Texas Ranger JoseCansecopitches 8th inning in 15-1 loss to Red Sox, he gives up 3 runs on 2 hits & 3 walks, he damages his arm
Space Shuttle STS 77Endeavour 11), lands

SingerDavid Bowiejoins guitaristDavid Gilmouras special guest during encore at Royal Albert Hall, London concert; they perform "Arnold Layne" and "Comfortably Numb" in what becomes Bowie's final UK appearance
"1Q84" books 1 and 2 byHaruki Murakamiis first published in Japan, sells out the first day (book 3 published 2010)
Hong Kong student activist group Scholarism started byJoshua Wongand Ivan Lam
President Obamaapproves US military training of 'moderate' Syrian rebels to fight the regime ofBashar al-Assadand al Qaeda-linked groups
"Jurassic World", directed by Colin Trevorrow and starringChris Prattand Bryce Dallas Howard premieres in Paris
Muhammadu Buhariis sworn in as the President of Nigeria
Sepp Blatteris controversially elected to a fifth term as President of FIFA despite bribery scandals; resigns 5 days later
Tiger Woodsis arrested and charged with driving under the influence in Jupiter, Florida
ABC TV network cancels TV comedy "Roseanne" after its starRoseanne Barrposts a racist tweet
British politicianBoris Johnsonordered to appear in court over claims he lied to the public during Britain's Brexit campaign
Israel's Knesset calls a snap election afterBenjamin Netanyahufails to form a government

US Special Counsel Robert Mueller says charging PresidentDonald Trumpwith a crime was never an option as no legal means to charge a sitting president; and that his report does not exonerate the president
US PresidentJoe Bidenvisits site of Uvalde school massacre in Texas, as Justice Department announces a review into how law enforcement responded
PresidentCyril Ramaphosa's ANC government loses its majority in parliament in South African elections, for the first time since apartheid ended 30 years ago
Rhode Island becomes last of original 13 colonies ratifying US Constitution
Edmund Hillary(NZ) and Tenzing Norgay (Nepal) are the first to reach the summit of Mount Everest as part of a British expedition
Student pro-democracy protesters in Tiananmen Square, China construct a replica of the Statue of Liberty, naming it the Goddess of Democracy
Gottfried Heinrich Graf zu Pappenheim, Bavarian field marshal, born in Treuchtlingen, Bavaria (d. 1632)
Anne Marie Louise d'Orléans, Duchess of Montpensier (Grand Mademoiselle), born in Palais du Louvre, Paris (d. 1693)
Cornelis van Bijnkershoek, Dutch jurist who helped develop international law along positivist lines, born in Middelburg, Netherlands (d. 1743)
Humphry Ditton, English mathematician, born in Salisbury, England (d. 1715)
Louis-Jean-Marie Daubenton, French zoologist, born in Montbard, Côte-d'Or, France (d. 1800)
James FitzGerald, 1st Duke of Leinster, Irish politician, born in Drogheda, Louth, Ireland (d. 1773)
William Jackson [Jackson of Exeter], English composer and organist, born in Exeter, England (d. 1803)
Johann Gottfried Krebs, German composer, born in Zwickau, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1814)
Charles Adams, American lawyer, son of US president John Adams and younger brother of president John Quincy Adams, born in Braintree, Province of Massachusetts Bay, British America (d. 1800)
Princess Sophia of Gloucester, great-granddaughter of King George II of Great Britain and niece of King George III, born in Mayfair, Middlesex (d. 1844)
Pietro Romani, Italian conductor and composer, born in Rome, Roman Republic (d. 1877)
Johann Heinrich von Mädler, German astronomer (published the most complete map of the Moon of the time, Mappa Selenographica, 4 vol. with Wilhelm Beer), born in Berlin (d. 1874)
Erasmus Darwin Keyes, American banker, businessman and Major General (Union Army), born in Brinfield, Massachusetts (d. 1895)
Solomon Meredith, American politician and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Guilford County, North Carolina (d. 1875)
John H. Balsley, American carpenter and inventor (a practical folding wooden stepladder), born in Connellsville, Fayette County, Pennsylvania (d. 1895)
Cadmus M. Wilcox, American Major General (Confederate Army - American Civil War), born in Wayne County, North Carolina (d. 1890)
David Bell Birney, American businessman, lawyer, and Major General (Union Army), born in Huntsville, Alabama (d. 1864)
Ebenezer Butterick, American inventor (tissue paper dress pattern), born in Sterling, Massachusetts (d. 1903)
Reuben Lindsay Walker, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Logan, Virginia (d. 1890)
Alexander de Savornin Lohman, Dutch minister and party leader (CHU), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1924)
Luca Fumagalli, Italian pianist and composer (Luigi XI), born in Inzago, Lombardo-Venetian Kingdom, Austrian Empire (d. 1908)
Nat Thomson, Australian cricket all-rounder (2 Tests, 1 wicket, HS 41; NSW CA), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1896)
Ned Gregory, Australian cricket batsman (1 Test; first recognised Test in 1877), born in Sydney, New South Wales (d. 1899)
Sylvester Magee, last living American slave and oldest person who ever lived, born in North Carolina (d. 1971)
Émile Pessard, French pianist, concert and comic opera composer, and teacher (Maurice Ravel, Jacques Ibert), born in Paris (d. 1917)
Albert Apponyi, Hungarian politician (Minister of Religion and Education of Hungary), born in Vienna, Austrian Empire (d. 1933)
Heinrich Kàan-Albest, Bohemian pianist and composer, born in Ternopil, Austrian Empire (d. 1926)
Francesco Fanciulli, American-Italian composer, born in Porto Santo Stefano, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1915)
Isaac Albéniz, Spanish pianist, conductor, and composer (Suite española; Tango in D), born in Camprodon, Spain (d. 1909)
G. K. Chesterton [Gilbert Keith], English writer (Father Brown; The Everlasting Man), born in London (d. 1936)
Jan Ingenhoven, Dutch composer and conductor, born in Breda, Netherlands (d. 1951)
Oswald Spengler, German historian and philosopher (Decline of West), born in Blankenburg, Duchy of Brunswick, German Empire (d. 1936)
Frederik Septimus Kelly, Australian-British composer and rower (Summer Olympic gold, 1908), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1916)
Doris Ulmann, American photographer best known for her portraits of the people of Appalachia, born in New York City (d. 1934)
William Beatton Moonie, Scottish composer (The Weird of Colbar; Echoes of Perthshire), born in Stobo, Pebbleshire, Scotland (d. 1961)
Erwin Finlay-Freundlich, British astronomer (theory of relativity), born in Biebrich, Germany (d. 1964)
Francis de Bourguignon, Belgian pianist (accompanist to Nellie Melba), and contemporary classical composer, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1961)
Martin Wickramasinghe, Sri Lankan author (Gamperaliya - The Uprooted/Changing Village), born in Koggala, British Ceylon (d. 1976)
Alfonsina Storni, Argentine poet (La inquietud del rosal), born in Sala Capriasca, Switzerland (d. 1938)
Beatrice Lillie, Canadian comedian and actress (Around World in 80 Days), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1989)
Josef von Sternberg, Austrian-American director (Der blaue Engel), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1969)
George L. Funke, Dutch botanist (Flower Physiology), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1947)
Erich Wolfgang Korngold, Austrian-American composer (Violanta; The Adventures of Robin Hood), born in Brünn, Austria-Hungary (d. 1957)
Ignace Lilien, Austrian-Dutch composer, poet, and chemical engineer, born in Lemberg, Austria-Hungary (d. 1964)[1]
Hubert Opperman, Australian road cyclist, politician (multi-distance world records), born in Rochester, Victoria (d. 1996)
Robert Knox, British bacteriologist known for his contributions to antibiotic therapy, born in England (d. 2000)[1]
Ronald Russell, British journalist, author and Conservative politician, born in Seahouses, Northumberland (d. 1974)
Fela Sowande, Nigerian organist, orchestral, secular and sacred choral music composer, ethno-musicologist, and educator, born in Abeokuta, Nigeria (d. 1987)
Johannes "Jan" Teulings, Dutch actor (Lentelied, The Three Wishes), born in Hilversum, Netherlands (d. 1989)
Sebastian Shaw, British actor, director, playwright and poet (High Season, Ace of Spades, Caste), born in Holt, England (d. 1994)
Desmond Shawe-Taylor, British critic and writer (co-author of The Record Guide), born in Dublin (d. 1995)
Hartland Molson, Canadian businessman (Molson family), senator and sports executive (Montreal Canadiens), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2002)
Dick Metz, American golfer (US Open 1938 runner-up; 10 PGA Tour titles), born in Arkansas City, Kansas (d. 1993)
Neil R. Jones, American sci-fi author (Space War, Twin Worlds), born in Fulton, New York (d. 1988)
Ralph Metcalfe, American athlete, politician (Rep-D-Ill; joint world record 100m, Olympic gold 4x100m relay 1936), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1978)
James Marjoribanks, British ambassador to the European Economic Community, born in Colinton, Scotland (d. 2002)
Ralph Louis Wain, British agricultural scientist, born in Hyde, Cheshire (d. 2000)
Iris Adrian, American actress (Blue Hawaii, Bluebeard), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1994)
Jan Hanlo, Dutch poet (Go to the Mosque), born in Bandung, Dutch East Indies (d. 1969)
Lucie Aubrac [Bernard], history teacher and French resistance member, born in Paris, France (d. 2007)
Douglas Black, Scottish physician and medical scientist (played a key role in the development of the National Health Service), born in Delting, Shetland (d. 2002)
Tony Zale, American boxer (World Middleweight Champion 1940-47, 48), born in Gary, Indiana (d. 1997)
Stacy Keach, Sr., American actor (Pretty Woman; The Parallax View), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2003)
David Jenkins, Welsh librarian (National Library of Wales), born in Blaenclydach, Glamorgan (d. 2002)
Igor Buketoff, American conductor (Iceland Symphony Orchestra, 1964-65), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2001)
Karl Münchinger, German conductor (Stuttgart Chamber Orchestra, 1945-87), born in Stuttgart, Germany (d. 1990)
Carl Story, American bluegrass musician (Rambling Mountaineers), born in Lenoir, North Carolina (d. 1995)
Herb Shriner, American humorist and TV host (Herb Shriner Show), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 1970)
Isabel Dean, English actress (Five Days One Summer Virgin Island, Ransom), born in Aldridge, Staffordshire (d. 1997)
Clifton James, American actor (Live and Let Die, Silver Streak), born in Spokane, Washington (d. 2017)
John Harsanyi, Hungarian-American economist (Nobel Prize in Economics, 1994, for game theory), and educator (University of California, Berkeley, 1964-90), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2000)[1]
Robin Haydon, British diplomat, born in Wandsworth, England (d. 1999)
George Terry, one of the youngest British chief constables in English history, born in Sussex, England (d. 1995)
Iannis Xenakis, Greek-French composer, music theorist, and architect who helped revolutionize 20th century classical music (Metastaseis), born in Brăila, Romania (d. 2001)
Joe Weatherly, American auto racer (NASCAR Sprint Cup champion 1962-63), born in Norfolk, Virginia (d. 1964)
Eugene Wright, American jazz bassist (The Dave Brubeck Quartet), sometimes known as "The Senator", born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2020)[1]
John Parker, 6th Earl of Morley, British peer and soldier, born in Saltram House, Devon (d. 2015)
Richard Worsley, British Army officer, born in Ballywalter, County Down, Northern Ireland (d. 2013)
Miloslav Kříž, Czech basketball coach (Czech Women's team, German Men's team), born in Prague, Czechoslovakia (d. 2013)
Pepper Paire, American National Women's Baseball Hall of Fame catcher (1948 AAGPBL All-Star Team), born in Los Angeles, CA (d. 2013)

Danny Davis [George Nowlan], American session trumpeter, pop-country music bandleader (The Nashville Brass - "I Saw the Light"), singer (Speed Racer theme), and record producer (Connie Francis), born in Dorchester, Massachusetts (d. 2008)
Kenneth Couzens, British Deputy Chairman of British Coal Board and significant figure during Miners Strike, born in Portsmouth, England (d. 2004)
Abdoulaye Wade, Senegalese politician (President of Senegal, 2000-12; Secretary-General of Senegalese Democratic Party, 1974-present), born in Kébémer, French Senegal
Charles Denner, French actor (The Bride Wore Black, The Man Who Loved Women), born in Tarnów, Poland (d. 1995)
Katie Boyle [Lady Sander Saunders], Italian born British actress and broadcaster (Eurovision), born in Florence, Italy (d. 2018)
Carl Toms, British stage designer (1 Million BC, Moon Zero Two), born in Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1999)
Dick Hillenius, Dutch biologist and writer, born in Amsterdam (d. 1987)
Trevor Holdsworth, English CEO (National Power), born in Bradford, Yorkshire, England (d. 2010)
Felix Rohatyn, American investment banker (NYC Municipal Assistance Corporation (Big MAC) bonds), and diplomat (US Ambassador to France, 1997-2000), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2019)
Freddie Redd, American hard-bop jazz pianist, organist, and composer, born in Harlem, New York (d. 2021)
Sandy Mosse, American jazz tenor saxophonist, and bandleader (Pieces of Eight), born in Detroit, Michigan, (d. 1983)
Peter Higgs, English theoretical physicist (Nobel Prize for Physics, 2013), born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 2024)[1]
Eddy Choong, Malaysian badminton player (Thomas Cup 1955), born in Penang, British Malaya (d. 2013)
Osvaldo Héctor Cruz, Argentine soccer forward (22 caps; Independiente	235 games), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 2023)
Paul R. Ehrlich, American biologist and author who founded the group Zero Population Growth, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Richie Guerin, American basketball Hall of Fame guard (6-time NBA All Star), born in New York City
Helmuth Rilling, German conductor (founder of the Gächinger Kantorei), born in Stuttgart, Germany
Tarquinio Provini, Italian motorcycle road racer (2-time world champion 1957-58), born in Roveleto di Cadeo, Emilia-Romagna (d. 2005)
